claims.text: On April 13th, 2029, the asteroid Apophis will come within 19,000 miles of Earth — flying under our communication satellites.

evidence.quote:
an asteroid named
Apous will come within 19,000 mi of
Earth

claims.rationale: 99942 Apophis's 2029 close approach is a well-documented NASA prediction: current JPL numbers give a miss distance of ~31,000 km (~19,800 mi) at 19:46 UT on April 13, 2029 — inside geosynchronous orbit altitude (~36,000 km). Host's number is accurate.

claims.text: When first discovered in December 2004, asteroid 2004 MN4 (Apophis) had a 2.7% chance — 1 in 37 — of hitting Earth on April 13th, 2029.

evidence.quote:
2004 MN4 had a 2.7% chance
of hitting Earth on April 13th, 2029

claims.rationale: The December 23-27, 2004 impact-probability spike to 2.7% (1 in 37) is well-documented in NASA/JPL Sentry archives and contemporaneous news. It remains the highest impact probability ever calculated for a sizeable object.

dependencies.rationale: Explains the historical origin of the 2029 Apophis prediction that opens the episode.

claims.text: Apophis was rated 4 on the Torino scale — the first object in history to reach that level.

evidence.quote: rated a four on the Torino scale

Apous was the first
object in history to reach that level

claims.rationale: In December 2004 Apophis reached Torino 4 — still the all-time high on that scale. Documented by IAU/JPL. Every other asteroid before and since has peaked at Torino 2 or below.

dependencies.rationale: The Torino 4 rating is the formal encoding of that same 2.7% impact probability on the IAU hazard scale.

claims.text: In 2005 scientists announced Apophis would miss Earth in 2029, but identified a half-mile-wide gravitational keyhole that would guarantee a 2036 impact if the asteroid passed through it.

evidence.quote:
In 2005, they announced Apous
would miss the Earth

A region in space about
half a mile wide

claims.rationale: The 2005 downgrade to 'miss in 2029' and the identification of the 2036-keyhole (Chesley, Chodas 2006) are documented. Subsequent radar tracking (2021 Arecibo/Goldstone) ruled out keyhole passage for the next century. 'Half-mile wide' is a reasonable approximation of the Apophis keyhole width (~600-800 m in most references).

dependencies.rationale: [logical] Downgrades the 2029 impact probability identified at discovery but introduces a new 2036 scenario.

claims.text: Current data from Goldstone and Arecibo radar shows Apophis will miss the 2029 keyhole, eliminating the 2036 impact threat.

evidence.quote: Fact: Apous is no longer a threat

claims.rationale: Host explicitly puts this in the verdict section as 'fact'. Confirmed by NASA JPL in March 2021 based on 2021 Arecibo/Goldstone radar tracking: Apophis is safely removed from the Sentry risk list for at least 100 years.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Refined radar tracking confirmed Apophis misses the keyhole, retiring the 2036 impact scenario.

claims.text: NASA estimates there are 25,000 city-killer asteroids crossing Earth's orbit and has only found half of them.

evidence.quote:
NASA estimates there are
25,000 city killers crossing our orbit
They've only found half

claims.rationale: NASA's estimate of ~25,000 near-Earth objects ≥140 m (the 'city killer' size bin, assuming airburst-level damage to regional areas) is the current planetary-defense community figure. As of 2024, ~42% have been catalogued — close to 'half'. Host's number is broadly accurate; exact catalog fraction varies by survey update.

dependencies.rationale: Pivots from the debunked Apophis-specific threat to the broader population-level threat from undetected objects.

claims.text: In 2019 asteroid 2019 OK was spotted less than 24 hours before closest approach, missing Earth by only 40,000 miles with a potential yield 300 times the Hiroshima bomb.
entities: quantity:300 times the size of the hiroshima bomb

evidence.quote:
In 2019, asteroid 2019, okay,
was found

It missed Earth by only 40,000
mi

claims.rationale: 2019 OK flyby July 25, 2019 at 0.19 lunar distances (~73,000 km / ~45,000 mi) with discovery only ~24h prior is well-documented. Size estimate 57-130 m; 300x Hiroshima is on the high side for kinetic energy but plausible for the upper size estimate. Host's auto-caption mangled 'OK' as 'okay'.

dependencies.rationale: 2019 OK is the canonical example illustrating the city-killer detection gap.

claims.text: NASA's NEO Surveyor infrared telescope launches in 2027 and should find 90% of remaining city killers within 10 to 20 years.

evidence.quote:
NASA's NEO surveyor telescope is set to
launch in 2027

claims.rationale: NEO Surveyor (previously NEOCam) is confirmed for September 2027 launch at L1 Lagrange point; mission goal is to find ≥2/3 of remaining hazardous NEOs ≥140 m within 10 years. 'Within 10-20 years' and '90%' are close to but slightly more optimistic than the stated mission objective.

dependencies.rationale: NEO Surveyor is the planned solution to the detection gap NASA has quantified.

claims.text: On September 26th, 2022, NASA's DART spacecraft slammed into the asteroid Dimorphos at 14,000 mph, changing its orbital period 25 times more than required.

evidence.quote:
NASA's Dark
spacecraft slammed into the asteroid
Dorphus at 14,000 mph

Dart changed the
asteroid's orbital period 25 times more
than required

claims.rationale: DART/Dimorphos impact September 26, 2022 at ~22,500 km/h (~14,000 mph) is well-documented. The orbital-period change was 33 minutes vs. the ~73-second mission-success threshold — roughly 25x, as host states. A landmark planetary-defense demonstration.

dependencies.rationale: DART demonstrates that — once city killers are detected — kinetic deflection is a viable response.

claims.text: The Outer Space Treaty of 1967 prohibits placing nuclear weapons in space, which limits our options for stopping a surprise city-killer.

evidence.quote:
The Outer Space Treaty
of 1967 prohibits placing nuclear
weapons in space

claims.rationale: Outer Space Treaty Article IV prohibits placing nuclear weapons or WMDs in orbit, on celestial bodies, or stationed in outer space. Signed January 27, 1967. 'Placing' is the precise legal term — a single-use emergency deflection is legally murky but technically permitted under some readings.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] DART succeeded conventionally, but the short-notice fallback — nuclear deflection — is constrained by the 1967 treaty regime.

claims.text: 12,800 years ago a ~62-mile-wide comet broke apart and bombarded Earth, triggering the Younger Dryas cold period and causing mass extinction of 75% of North American megafauna and two-thirds of the human population.

evidence.quote:
12,800 years ago, Earth was emerging
from the last ice age

A comet about 62 mi wide broke
apart and bombarded the planet

75% of North American
megapana went extinct

claims.rationale: The Younger Dryas Impact Hypothesis (Firestone et al. 2007, Kennett group) is a genuine published hypothesis but is contested. Mainstream paleoclimatology favors meltwater pulse / thermohaline circulation changes. 75% megafauna loss is accurate but causation remains debated. The '62-mile-wide comet' is on the larger end of proposed values. Host presents it as 'overwhelming' evidence, which overstates scientific consensus.

dependencies.rationale: If a comet already reset civilization once in human memory, the city-killer population represents an existential class of threat.

claims.text: The most dangerous known comet is Swift-Tuttle, 16 miles wide, with land-impact energy 27 times greater than the Chicxulub dinosaur-killer.

evidence.quote:
most dangerous comet
we know is Swift Tuttle, 16 mi wide

claims.rationale: 109P/Swift-Tuttle is ~26 km (~16 mi) in diameter (Yeomans et al.) and parent body of the Perseids. Its high velocity (60 km/s) and size give it massive kinetic energy; '27x Chicxulub' is plausible arithmetic for kinetic energy ratio but the Chicxulub impactor was ~10 km at lower velocity, so actual ratio depends on assumptions. Current orbital calculations rule out impact for ≥1000 years.

dependencies.rationale: Names a specific comet whose size and orbital characteristics illustrate the ongoing extinction-scale cometary threat.

claims.text: Comets of all sizes hit the Earth on average every 3 days.

evidence.quote:
Comets of all sizes hit the
Earth on average every 3 days

claims.rationale: This is very likely false as stated. Dust-grain cometary debris burns up daily in meteor showers, but 'comets' as in nucleus fragments hitting Earth every 3 days is orders of magnitude too frequent. Documented comet impacts (Tunguska 1908, Chelyabinsk 2013 if cometary) are decadal-to-centennial events. Host is conflating meteor-shower dust with comet-scale impacts.

claims.text: The 1908 Tunguska event — a 200-foot fragment exploding over Siberia during peak Torids — flattened 80 million trees.

evidence.quote: Tungusa event in 1908

claims.rationale: Tunguska 1908 is well-documented: ~60-80 million trees flattened over ~2,150 km². Object estimated 50-80 m (not 200 ft / 60 m — reasonable match). 'During peak of the Torids' is contested — the Taurid connection is a minority hypothesis advanced by Asher & Clube; most Tunguska researchers favor an asteroidal bolide.

claims.text: In late 2024 asteroid 2024 YR4 briefly became the most dangerous object in modern history with impact probability climbing past 3%, prompting China to establish its first planetary-defense unit.

evidence.quote:
asteroid 2024 YR4 became the
most dangerous object in modern history

claims.rationale: 2024 YR4 (discovered December 27, 2024) peaked at ~3.1% 2032 impact probability in February 2025 — the highest since Apophis's 2004 peak, making it the most dangerous NEO since Apophis for a brief window. Probability dropped to near zero with additional observations. China's 2024 announcement of a planetary-defense unit under CNSA is contemporaneous.
